title: The Living Document
narrativeIntro: There's a layer beneath the code. A structure that the code manipulates -- a document that lives and changes. Nodes, elements, a tree of content that renders into something visible. The Drift shows you this layer now: the interface between logic and perception. What you code here becomes what you see.
narrativeOutro: You can see it now -- the bridge between code and form. Everything visible is a node in a tree, and every node can be reached, changed, created, destroyed. The interface is yours to shape.
phmConnectionNote: Ryland interacts with the ship's interfaces more directly. You learn the DOM -- the living structure of a webpage.
conceptsPreview: DOM manipulation, element creation, and event handling

challenges.title: Query
challenges.narrativeHook: You query the document for an element. What does it return?
challenges.instructions:
Given this HTML, predict what the two `console.log` calls will output: ```html
Online

System ready

``` The first query finds an element that exists. The second queries for an id that doesn't exist anywhere in the document.
challenges.predictPrompt: What two values will be logged?

explainGate.prompt: Why does querySelector return null for a missing element instead of throwing an error?

hints: .textContent returns the text inside an element, not the element's HTML tag.
querySelector returns null when no matching element is found -- it doesn't throw.
null is different from undefined: querySelector always returns either an element or null, never undefined.

challenges.title: Create and Attach
challenges.narrativeHook: Create an element, give it content, attach it to the tree.
challenges.instructions:
Build a paragraph element from scratch and add it to the page: 1. Create a `

` element 2. Set its text content to `"Signal received"` 3. Append it to `document.body`

tests.description:
A

element should exist in the body
tests.testCode:
const p = document.querySelector('p'); if (!p) throw new Error('No

element found in document.body');
const p = document.querySelector('p'); if (p.textContent !== 'Signal received') throw new Error(`Paragraph text should be 'Signal received', got '${p.textContent}'`);

hints:
document.createElement('p') creates a new

element -- it's not in the page yet.
element.textContent = 'text' sets the text content of an element.
document.body.appendChild(element) attaches the element to the page so it becomes visible.

challenges.title: Missing Connection
challenges.narrativeHook: The element should appear but doesn't. Something in the DOM wiring is wrong.
challenges.instructions:
This code should update the display element to show 'Active', but it crashes instead. The HTML has a div with `id="display"`.

There is exactly one bug. Find it and fix it.

tests.testCode: const el = document.getElementById('display'); if (!el || el.textContent !== 'Active') throw new Error('Display element should contain the text Active');

explainGate.prompt: What was the typo and why is it so easy to miss?

hints: Read the method name letter by letter -- is it exactly 'getElementById'?
Compare: getElementByld (what's written) vs getElementById (the real method). Look at the 'I' vs 'l'.
The lowercase letter 'l' was used instead of the uppercase letter 'I' in 'Id'. Change getElementByld to getElementById.

challenges.title: Style and Modify
challenges.narrativeHook: Change an element's text, its style, its attributes. Make it respond.
challenges.instructions:
Given a div with `id="panel"`, make three modifications:

1. Change its background color to `"#1E1B35"`
2. Add the CSS class `"active"` to it
3. Set a custom data attribute: `data-status="online"`

tests.description: Panel background color should be set
tests.testCode: const p = document.getElementById('panel'); if (!p.style.backgroundColor) throw new Error('Background color was not set on panel');
const p = document.getElementById('panel'); if (!p.classList.contains('active')) throw new Error('Panel is missing the active CSS class');
const p = document.getElementById('panel'); if (p.getAttribute('data-status') !== 'online') throw new Error(`data-status should be 'online', got '${p.getAttribute('data-status')}'`);

hints: element.style.backgroundColor = 'color' sets an inline style. Note: camelCase, not kebab-case.
element.classList.add('className') adds a CSS class without removing existing ones.
element.setAttribute('data-status', 'online') sets any attribute on the element.

challenges.title: Interactive Element
challenges.narrativeHook: Build a small interactive element from scratch: HTML, style, and behavior.
challenges.instructions: Build a click counter from scratch. The page starts empty. 1. Create a `

tests.description: A button with text 'Click me' should exist
tests.testCode: const btn = document.querySelector('button'); if (!btn || btn.textContent !== 'Click me') throw new Error('Button missing or has wrong text');

tests.description: A paragraph showing 'Clicks: 0' should exist initially
tests.testCode: const p = document.querySelector('p'); if (!p || !p.textContent.includes('Clicks: 0')) throw new Error('Paragraph missing or wrong initial text');

tests.description: Clicking the button should increment the counter
tests.testCode: const btn = document.querySelector('button'); btn.click(); const p = document.querySelector('p'); if (!p.textContent.includes('1')) throw new Error('Counter did not increment after one click');

const btn = document.querySelector('button'); btn.click(); btn.click(); btn.click(); const p = document.querySelector('p'); if (!p.textContent.includes('3')) throw new Error('Counter should reach 3 after three clicks');

explainGate.prompt: How does addEventListener connect a user interaction to code execution?

hints: document.createElement creates elements, .textContent sets their text, .appendChild adds them to the page.
button.addEventListener('click', function) registers a function to run each time the button is clicked.
Inside the click handler, increment clickCount and update display.textContent using a template literal: `Clicks: ${clickCount}`
